participants.extracted: The study enrolled 10 preterm infants from a single-family-room NICU in an academic health center. Infants were less than 37 weeks’ gestation at birth, less than 1 week old, intubated, on conventional ventilation, and receiving inline endotracheal suctioning. All infants received both the routine suctioning condition and the four-handed care condition in a randomized crossover design. The sample was 40% female and 70% African American, and all participants were non-Hispanic. Mean birth weight was 1014 g and mean gestational age was 27.0 weeks; infants were excluded if they had chromosomal or genetic abnormalities, congenital heart disease, dysmorphic syndrome, or were receiving paralytics, analgesics, or sedating medications.

interventions.extracted: This crossover study tested four-handed care during endotracheal tube suctioning in preterm mechanically ventilated neonates. Four-handed care consisted of a second trained caregiver (research assistant) providing behavioral and physical support to the infant during the procedure by opening the incubator portholes, using gentle touch, containing and flexing the infant’s limbs, supporting positioning, and helping the infant achieve a calm, regulated state while also assisting the bedside clinician as needed during suctioning. The infant was monitored until physiologic and behavioral self-regulation was re-established, up to 10 minutes. The comparison condition was routine suctioning care performed by a sole caregiver without the added four-handed supportive interventions.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure physiologic outcomes including heart rate (HR) and oxygen saturation (SpO2) continuously by ECG and pulse oximetry at three time points: a presuctioning baseline period of 10 minutes before suctioning, during the suctioning procedure, and a postsuctioning recovery period of 10 minutes after suctioning. Stress was planned to be measured with salivary cortisol collected at baseline before suctioning and again 30 minutes after suctioning. Behavioral outcomes included behavioral state assessed with the Anderson Behavioral State Scale (ABSS) at an undisturbed pre-observation baseline, before suctioning, during suctioning, and after suctioning over repeated intervals. Stress and defense behaviors were to be counted before, during, and after suctioning. Self-regulatory and approach behaviors were also to be counted before, during, and after suctioning. Recovery outcomes were planned as time for HR and SpO2 to return to within 10% of baseline, percent of time HR and SpO2 were outside the normal/baseline range during and after suctioning, and the number and type of monitor call-backs/alarms recorded during the 10-minute postprocedure recovery period.
